sections.title: Definitions

items: Pharmacology: Study of substances interacting with living systems through chemical processes.
Medical Pharmacology: Use of drugs to prevent, diagnose, and treat disease.
Toxicology: Study of harmful effects of chemicals on living systems.

sections.imageCaption: FIGURE 1-1 — Major areas of study in pharmacology

sections.title: Major Divisions

blocks.label: A. Pharmacodynamics
items: What the drug does to the body:

blocks.label: B. Pharmacokinetics
items: What the body does to the drug:

sections.imageCaption: FIGURE 1-1 — Major areas of study in pharmacology

items: Drugs may be solid, liquid, or gas.
Many small-molecule drugs are weak acids or weak bases.
Most small-molecule drugs have molecular weight about 100–1000 Da; larger biologics usually need specialized delivery or extracellular targets.

blocks.label: Why this table matters
items: Kd is inversely related to receptor affinity.
Carvedilol enantiomers bind beta receptors very differently.
This shows how drug shape/stereochemistry can change pharmacologic effect.

sections.title: Drug-Receptor Interactions

blocks.label: Drug Types
items: Antagonist → binds without activating and reduces agonist effect
Partial agonist → activates receptor with lower maximal effect than a full agonist
Inverse agonist → reduces constitutive receptor activity

sections.imageSupplementCaption: FIGURE 1-3 — Agonist, antagonist & inverse agonist model

blocks.label: Drug movement mechanisms (FIGURE 1-4)
items: C — Carrier-mediated transport (incl. transporter uptake/efflux; see TABLE 1-3)

blocks.label: Fick's Law
blocks.note: Diffusion increases with gradient, area, and permeability, and decreases as membrane thickness increases.

sections.title: Weak Acids & Weak Bases

blocks.content:
The Henderson–Hasselbalch equation predicts how much of a weak acid or weak base is ionized at a given pH.

```text
Weak acid:  pH = pKa + log([A⁻] / [HA])
Weak base:  pH = pKa + log([BH⁺] / [B])
```

Ionized vs unionized forms affect absorption, distribution, and renal elimination.

blocks.label: Clinical Importance
items: Weak acids excreted faster in alkaline urine.
Weak bases excreted faster in acidic urine.

blocks.content: Aspirin overdose treated with sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine.

sections.imageCaption: TABLE 1-4 — Ionization constants (pKa) of common drugs
sections.imageSupplementCaption: FIGURE 1-5 — Ion trapping of weak bases in urine

blocks.label: Clinical Trial Phases
items: Phase 1: Initial human safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetics; usually healthy volunteers, but patients may be used for high-risk drugs
Phase 2: Dose-ranging and preliminary efficacy in patients
Phase 3: Larger confirmatory trials of efficacy and safety
Phase 4: Postmarketing safety monitoring and additional studies

sections.title: FDA Regulation

blocks.content: Modern FDA approval requires substantial evidence of effectiveness and an acceptable safety profile; the 1938 act emphasized safety and the 1962 amendments added efficacy requirements.

sections.imageCaption: TABLE 1-6 — Major drug legislation in the USA

items: Drug interactions can arise from pharmacodynamic effects or pharmacokinetic transport/metabolism changes.
Drugs or biologics intended to diagnose, prevent, or treat rare diseases.
In the US, rare disease generally means fewer than 200,000 affected people, or a condition meeting the act's cost-recovery provisions.
Supported by the Orphan Drug Act (1983), which provides development incentives and market exclusivity.
All substances can be toxic depending on dose.
Drug selectivity depends on receptor binding.
Pharmacology integrates physiology, biochemistry, genetics, and pathology.
Large-molecule therapeutics and monoclonal antibodies have become an important part of modern drug approvals.
